title: Changing Tides for American Express: Corporate Comp Plans
content:
When Stephen Squeri took the helm at American Express, he quickly realized that the company's compensation plan needed a radical overhaul. He completely junked the old practice that fostered internal competition and replaced it with a unified approach. Squeri told his executive team that they were all going to sink or swim together, tying their bonuses entirely to the overall company's performance. This shift ensured that when executives sat in a room together, their focus was solely on what was best for American Express. The new strategy successfully eliminated selfish infighting and aligned the leadership toward a common goal.

The results of Squeri’s leadership have been nothing short of a financial miracle for the historic credit card company. Despite facing intense competition from aggressive, venture-backed startups, American Express managed to hike revenues at an impressive annual clip. By maintaining strict control over overall expenses, the company achieved an operating leverage that drove massive earnings per share. Industry watchers marvel at the fact that customers willingly pay nearly nine hundred dollars in annual fees for premium cards. Squeri’s disciplined approach proved that a legacy brand could still dominate in a rapidly changing financial landscape.

However, the worsening macroeconomic picture poses a significant threat to the company's continued success. While the affluent consumer base has been thriving, the broader labor market is showing clear signs of softening. If rising joblessness reverses the favorable credit cycle, it could trigger increased charge-offs and severely hit profits. Furthermore, if artificial intelligence wipes out wide swaths of white-collar jobs, the collateral damage will fall squarely on Amex’s core customers. The company must now navigate these treacherous economic waters while sustaining its remarkable growth streak.
